---
title: "Ntracker: Mathematical Methods for Commodity Price Signal Analysis"
subtitle: "Decision Support for Australian Growers"
author:
  - name: "CSIRO"
date: today
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    papersize: a4
    fontsize: 11pt
    geometry:
      - margin=2.5cm
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: "csiroocean"
    citecolor: "csiroteal"
    urlcolor: "csiroorange"
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    fig-pos: "htbp"
    header-includes: |
      \usepackage{amsmath,amssymb,amsthm}
      \usepackage{mathtools}
      \usepackage{tikz}
      \usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta,positioning,calc,decorations.pathmorphing,shapes.geometric,patterns,backgrounds}
      \usepackage{pgfplots}
      \pgfplotsset{compat=1.18}
      \usepackage{booktabs}
      \usepackage{algorithm2e}
      \usepackage{xcolor}

      % CSIRO Colour Palette
      \definecolor{csiromidday}{HTML}{00A9CE}
      \definecolor{csiromidnight}{HTML}{00313C}
      \definecolor{csiroocean}{HTML}{004B87}
      \definecolor{csiroteal}{HTML}{007377}
      \definecolor{csiroorange}{HTML}{E87722}
      \definecolor{csirogold}{HTML}{FFB81C}
      \definecolor{csirolavender}{HTML}{9FAEE5}
      \definecolor{csirosteel}{HTML}{757579}
      \definecolor{csiromist}{HTML}{DADBDC}
      \definecolor{csiroteallight}{HTML}{36CCD3}

      % Math environments
      \newtheorem{definition}{Definition}[section]
      \newtheorem{proposition}{Proposition}[section]
      \newtheorem{remark}{Remark}[section]

      % Notation shortcuts
      \newcommand{\E}{\mathbb{E}}
      \newcommand{\Var}{\operatorname{Var}}
      \newcommand{\Cov}{\operatorname{Cov}}
      \newcommand{\Corr}{\operatorname{Corr}}
      \newcommand{\R}{\mathbb{R}}
      \newcommand{\N}{\mathbb{N}}
      \newcommand{\indicator}{\mathbf{1}}
      \newcommand{\veps}{\varepsilon}
bibliography: references.bib
csl: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/citation-style-language/styles/master/apa.csl
---

\newpage

# Introduction {#sec-intro}

Commodity price dynamics directly impact the profitability and planning
decisions of Australian growers. Fertiliser (urea), energy (Brent crude oil),
and grain (wheat) prices are interconnected through global supply chains:
crude oil and natural gas drive urea manufacturing costs, which in turn
influence grain production costs and farm-gate economics.

**Ntracker** is a real-time commodity price dashboard designed to provide
Australian growers with actionable decision support. This document provides
the mathematical foundations underlying each analytical component of the
system, and introduces extensions from stochastic calculus, signal processing,
and time series econometrics that offer promising avenues for enhanced
forecasting.

We denote commodity prices as $\{P_t^{(i)}\}_{t \geq 0}$ for commodity
$i \in \{\text{oil}, \text{wheat}, \text{urea}\}$, observed at discrete
times $t = 1, 2, \ldots, T$. Log-prices are written $X_t^{(i)} = \log P_t^{(i)}$,
and log-returns as $r_t^{(i)} = X_t^{(i)} - X_{t-1}^{(i)} = \log(P_t^{(i)} / P_{t-1}^{(i)})$.


# System Architecture {#sec-architecture}

\begin{figure}[htbp]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  node distance=1.2cm and 2.0cm,
  block/.style={rectangle, draw=csiromidnight, fill=csiromidnight!8,
    rounded corners=4pt, minimum width=3cm, minimum height=1cm,
    font=\small, text=csiromidnight, align=center},
  data/.style={rectangle, draw=csiroorange, fill=csiroorange!8,
    rounded corners=4pt, minimum width=2.5cm, minimum height=0.8cm,
    font=\small, text=csiromidnight, align=center},
  signal/.style={rectangle, draw=csiroteal, fill=csiroteal!8,
    rounded corners=4pt, minimum width=3cm, minimum height=1cm,
    font=\small\bfseries, text=csiroteal, align=center},
  arr/.style={-{Stealth[length=3mm]}, thick, color=csiroocean}
]
% Data sources
\node[data] (yahoo) {Yahoo Finance\\[-2pt]\scriptsize Brent, Wheat, AUD};
\node[data, below=0.6cm of yahoo] (wb) {World Bank\\[-2pt]\scriptsize Urea (Pink Sheet)};

% Processing
\node[block, right=2cm of $(yahoo)!0.5!(wb)$] (preproc) {Preprocessing\\[-2pt]\scriptsize Align, resample,\\[-2pt]\scriptsize merge, AUD convert};

% Analysis
\node[block, right=2cm of preproc] (analysis) {Analysis\\[-2pt]\scriptsize Z-score, CCF,\\[-2pt]\scriptsize Rolling $\rho$, VAR};

% Signals
\node[signal, above right=0.4cm and 2cm of analysis] (alert) {\color{csiroteal}Traffic Light\\[-2pt]\scriptsize Percentile alerts};
\node[signal, right=2cm of analysis] (forecast) {\color{csiroteal}Forecast\\[-2pt]\scriptsize 6-month outlook};
\node[signal, below right=0.4cm and 2cm of analysis] (leadlag) {\color{csiroteal}Lead-Lag\\[-2pt]\scriptsize Early warnings};

% Arrows
\draw[arr] (yahoo.east) -- ++(0.5,0) |- (preproc.west);
\draw[arr] (wb.east) -- ++(0.5,0) |- (preproc.west);
\draw[arr] (preproc) -- (analysis);
\draw[arr] (analysis.east) -- ++(0.5,0) |- (alert.west);
\draw[arr] (analysis.east) -- (forecast.west);
\draw[arr] (analysis.east) -- ++(0.5,0) |- (leadlag.west);
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{Ntracker data pipeline: from public APIs through statistical
analysis to grower-facing decision signals.}
\label{fig:pipeline}
\end{figure}


# Current Methods {#sec-current}

## Normalised Comparison (Z-Score) {#sec-zscore}

To compare commodities with different units and scales (USD/barrel vs
USD/mt vs USc/bushel), we standardise each series to zero mean and unit
variance:

\begin{definition}[Z-Score Normalisation]
For commodity $i$ with price series $\{P_t^{(i)}\}_{t=1}^{T}$, the
normalised series is
\begin{equation}
  z_t^{(i)} = \frac{P_t^{(i)} - \bar{P}^{(i)}}{\hat{\sigma}^{(i)}},
  \qquad
  \bar{P}^{(i)} = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} P_t^{(i)},
  \qquad
  \hat{\sigma}^{(i)} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T-1}\sum_{t=1}^{T}
    \bigl(P_t^{(i)} - \bar{P}^{(i)}\bigr)^2}.
  \label{eq:zscore}
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

The resulting dimensionless series $z_t^{(i)} \in \R$ enables direct visual
comparison of co-movements across commodities on a common axis.


## Cross-Correlation Analysis (Lead-Lag) {#sec-ccf}

The cross-correlation function (CCF) quantifies the linear dependence
between two time series at different temporal offsets, enabling identification
of *lead-lag* relationships---i.e., which commodity's price moves first.

\begin{definition}[Sample Cross-Correlation Function]
For series $\{X_t\}$ and $\{Y_t\}$ of length $T$, the sample
cross-correlation at lag $\tau \in \mathbb{Z}$ is
\begin{equation}
  \hat{\rho}_{XY}(\tau) =
  \frac{\hat{\gamma}_{XY}(\tau)}
       {\sqrt{\hat{\gamma}_{XX}(0)\,\hat{\gamma}_{YY}(0)}},
  \label{eq:ccf}
\end{equation}
where the sample cross-covariance is
\begin{equation}
  \hat{\gamma}_{XY}(\tau) =
  \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T - |\tau|}
  \bigl(X_{t} - \bar{X}\bigr)
  \bigl(Y_{t+\tau} - \bar{Y}\bigr),
  \qquad \tau \in \{-\tau_{\max}, \ldots, \tau_{\max}\}.
  \label{eq:crosscov}
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

\begin{remark}[Interpretation for growers]
If $\hat{\rho}_{XY}(\tau)$ attains its maximum at $\tau^* > 0$, then $X$
\emph{leads} $Y$ by approximately $\tau^*$ periods. In the Ntracker context,
if Brent crude leads urea with $\tau^* \approx 3$ months, a spike in oil
prices today signals that urea prices are likely to follow within a quarter---
giving growers an actionable purchasing window.
\end{remark}


## Rolling Windowed Correlation {#sec-rolling}

Correlations between commodity prices are not stationary; they evolve with
market regimes. A rolling window of width $w$ captures this evolution:

\begin{equation}
  \hat{\rho}_w(t) =
  \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{s=t-w+1}^{t}
    \bigl(X_s - \bar{X}_{[t-w+1:t]}\bigr)
    \bigl(Y_s - \bar{Y}_{[t-w+1:t]}\bigr)}
  {\sqrt{\displaystyle\sum_{s=t-w+1}^{t}
    \bigl(X_s - \bar{X}_{[t-w+1:t]}\bigr)^2
    \sum_{s=t-w+1}^{t}
    \bigl(Y_s - \bar{Y}_{[t-w+1:t]}\bigr)^2}},
  \qquad t \geq w,
  \label{eq:rolling}
\end{equation}

where $\bar{X}_{[a:b]} = \frac{1}{b-a+1}\sum_{s=a}^{b} X_s$.

In Ntracker, we use $w = 12$ months. A sharp decline in $\hat{\rho}_w(t)$
between historically correlated commodities (e.g., oil and urea) signals a
**regime change**---often caused by supply disruptions, policy interventions,
or geopolitical events---and frequently precedes significant price movements.


## Percentile-Based Alert System {#sec-alerts}

The traffic light system maps each commodity's current price to its
empirical cumulative distribution function (ECDF) over the analysis window:

\begin{definition}[Percentile Rank]
\begin{equation}
  \Pi_t^{(i)} =
  \frac{1}{N}\sum_{s=1}^{N}
  \indicator\!\bigl(P_s^{(i)} < P_t^{(i)}\bigr) \times 100,
  \label{eq:percentile}
\end{equation}
where $N$ is the number of historical observations in the chosen window.
\end{definition}

The alert mapping is:

\begin{equation}
  \text{Alert}(P_t^{(i)}) =
  \begin{cases}
    \textcolor{green!60!black}{\text{\textsf{NORMAL}}}
      & \text{if } \Pi_t^{(i)} < 60, \\[4pt]
    \textcolor{csirogold}{\text{\textsf{WATCH}}}
      & \text{if } 60 \leq \Pi_t^{(i)} < 80, \\[4pt]
    \textcolor{red!70!black}{\text{\textsf{ELEVATED}}}
      & \text{if } \Pi_t^{(i)} \geq 80.
  \end{cases}
  \label{eq:alert}
\end{equation}


## Vector Autoregression (VAR) {#sec-var}

The VAR model [@sims1980; @lutkepohl2005] captures the joint dynamics of
multiple time series, allowing each variable to depend on its own past
values and the past values of all other variables.

\begin{definition}[VAR($p$) Model]
Let $\boldsymbol{r}_t = (r_t^{\text{oil}}, \, r_t^{\text{wheat}}, \,
r_t^{\text{urea}})^\top \in \R^3$ be the vector of monthly log-returns.
The VAR($p$) model is
\begin{equation}
  \boldsymbol{r}_t = \boldsymbol{c} + \sum_{\ell=1}^{p} \boldsymbol{A}_\ell \, \boldsymbol{r}_{t-\ell}
  + \boldsymbol{\veps}_t,
  \qquad
  \boldsymbol{\veps}_t \sim \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{0}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}),
  \label{eq:var}
\end{equation}
where $\boldsymbol{c} \in \R^3$ is an intercept vector,
$\boldsymbol{A}_\ell \in \R^{3 \times 3}$ are coefficient matrices, and
$\boldsymbol{\Sigma} \in \R^{3 \times 3}$ is the innovation covariance matrix.
\end{definition}

The lag order $p$ is selected by minimising the Akaike Information Criterion
[@akaike1974]:

\begin{equation}
  \text{AIC}(p) = \log \det(\hat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}_p)
  + \frac{2 \cdot 3^2 p}{T},
  \label{eq:aic}
\end{equation}

where $\hat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}_p$ is the ML estimate of the residual covariance
for a VAR($p$). We enforce $p \geq 1$.

### Forecasting

Given the fitted model, the $h$-step-ahead forecast is computed recursively:

\begin{equation}
  \hat{\boldsymbol{r}}_{T+h|T} = \boldsymbol{c}
  + \sum_{\ell=1}^{p} \boldsymbol{A}_\ell \, \hat{\boldsymbol{r}}_{T+h-\ell|T},
  \label{eq:var-forecast}
\end{equation}

with $\hat{\boldsymbol{r}}_{T+h-\ell|T} = \boldsymbol{r}_{T+h-\ell}$ when $T+h-\ell \leq T$.
The forecast is converted to price levels via cumulative log-returns:

\begin{equation}
  \hat{P}_{T+h}^{(i)} = P_T^{(i)} \cdot
  \exp\!\left(\sum_{j=1}^{h} \hat{r}_{T+j|T}^{(i)}\right).
  \label{eq:price-forecast}
\end{equation}

Confidence intervals are obtained from the forecast error variance decomposition
[@lutkepohl2005, Chapter 3], yielding an 80\% prediction interval
$[\hat{P}_{\text{lo}}, \hat{P}_{\text{hi}}]$ for each commodity.


\begin{figure}[htbp]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  var/.style={circle, draw=csiromidnight, fill=csiromidday!15,
    minimum size=1.4cm, font=\small\bfseries, text=csiromidnight},
  arr/.style={-{Stealth[length=2.5mm]}, thick},
  self/.style={-{Stealth[length=2.5mm]}, thick, looseness=5}
]
\node[var] (oil) at (0, 0) {Oil};
\node[var] (wheat) at (4, 0) {Wheat};
\node[var] (urea) at (2, -2.5) {Urea};

% Cross effects
\draw[arr, csiroorange] (oil) to[bend left=20]
  node[above, font=\scriptsize, csiroorange] {$A_{21}$} (wheat);
\draw[arr, csiroteal] (oil) to[bend left=20]
  node[left, font=\scriptsize, csiroteal] {$A_{31}$} (urea);
\draw[arr, csirolavender] (wheat) to[bend left=20]
  node[right, font=\scriptsize, csirolavender] {$A_{32}$} (urea);

% Reverse
\draw[arr, csiroorange!50] (wheat) to[bend left=20]
  node[below, font=\scriptsize, csiroorange!50] {$A_{12}$} (oil);
\draw[arr, csiroteal!50] (urea) to[bend left=20]
  node[right, font=\scriptsize, csiroteal!50] {$A_{13}$} (oil);
\draw[arr, csirolavender!50] (urea) to[bend left=20]
  node[left, font=\scriptsize, csirolavender!50] {$A_{23}$} (wheat);

% Self loops
\draw[self, csiromidday] (oil) to[out=135, in=45] (oil);
\draw[self, csiromidday] (wheat) to[out=135, in=45] (wheat);
\draw[self, csiromidday] (urea) to[out=-45, in=-135] (urea);
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{VAR interaction structure: each commodity depends on its own lagged
values (self-loops, \textcolor{csiromidday}{blue}) and cross-effects from
other commodities. Darker arrows indicate typically stronger effects;
lighter arrows indicate weaker reverse effects.}
\label{fig:var-structure}
\end{figure}


\newpage

# Theoretical Extensions {#sec-extensions}

The current Ntracker methods provide a solid foundation for grower decision
support. The following sections introduce mathematical frameworks from
stochastic calculus, signal processing, and econometrics that offer
deeper analytical capabilities.


## Ornstein--Uhlenbeck Mean Reversion {#sec-ou}

### Motivation

Commodity prices, unlike equity prices, exhibit **mean reversion**: when
oil prices spike due to a geopolitical shock, supply-side responses
(increased production, demand destruction, substitution) gradually pull
prices back toward long-run equilibrium levels determined by marginal
production costs [@schwartz1997; @pindyck1999].

This fundamental economic property makes Geometric Brownian Motion (GBM)---the
workhorse of equity option pricing---inappropriate for commodities. GBM has
no mechanism for reversion. Instead, we turn to the Ornstein--Uhlenbeck (O-U)
process [@uhlenbeck1930], which elegantly captures mean reversion via a
restoring drift.

\begin{definition}[Ornstein--Uhlenbeck Process]
The O-U process $\{X_t\}_{t \geq 0}$ satisfies the stochastic differential
equation (SDE)
\begin{equation}
  dX_t = \theta(\mu - X_t)\,dt + \sigma\,dW_t,
  \label{eq:ou-sde}
\end{equation}
where:
\begin{itemize}
  \item $\mu \in \R$ is the long-run equilibrium level,
  \item $\theta > 0$ is the speed of mean reversion,
  \item $\sigma > 0$ is the volatility (diffusion coefficient),
  \item $W_t$ is a standard Wiener process.
\end{itemize}
\end{definition}

### Analytic Solution

By the variation of constants formula (see @oksendal2013, Chapter 5), the
solution to \eqref{eq:ou-sde} is:

\begin{equation}
  X_t = \mu + (X_0 - \mu)\,e^{-\theta t}
  + \sigma \int_0^t e^{-\theta(t-s)}\,dW_s.
  \label{eq:ou-solution}
\end{equation}

The stochastic integral is Gaussian, giving us closed-form moments:

\begin{proposition}[O-U Conditional Distribution]
\label{prop:ou-moments}
Given $X_0 = x_0$, we have $X_t \mid X_0 = x_0 \sim \mathcal{N}(m_t, v_t)$, where
\begin{align}
  m_t &= \E[X_t \mid X_0 = x_0]
       = \mu + (x_0 - \mu)\,e^{-\theta t},
  \label{eq:ou-mean} \\[6pt]
  v_t &= \Var(X_t \mid X_0 = x_0)
       = \frac{\sigma^2}{2\theta}\bigl(1 - e^{-2\theta t}\bigr).
  \label{eq:ou-var}
\end{align}
\end{proposition}

\begin{remark}[Stationary distribution]
As $t \to \infty$, the conditional distribution converges to the stationary
distribution $X_\infty \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2 / 2\theta)$. The
rate of convergence is governed by $\theta$.
\end{remark}

### Half-Life of Mean Reversion

A crucial quantity for growers is the **half-life**---the expected time for
a price deviation to decay to half its initial magnitude:

\begin{equation}
  t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\theta}.
  \label{eq:halflife}
\end{equation}

For instance, if $\theta = 0.23\,\text{month}^{-1}$ for urea (estimated
from historical data), then $t_{1/2} \approx 3$ months, meaning urea
price deviations from equilibrium typically halve in about one quarter---a
directly actionable insight for purchasing decisions.


### Application to Commodity Log-Prices

Setting $X_t = \log P_t$, we model commodity prices as:

\begin{equation}
  d(\log P_t) = \theta\bigl(\mu - \log P_t\bigr)\,dt + \sigma\,dW_t,
  \label{eq:ou-logprice}
\end{equation}

which implies that $P_t$ follows a **geometric O-U process** (also known
as the exponential O-U or Vasicek model applied to log-prices; cf.
@vasicek1977). The price level itself satisfies (by It\^o's lemma):

\begin{equation}
  dP_t = P_t\!\left[\left(\theta(\mu - \log P_t)
  + \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma^2\right)dt + \sigma\,dW_t\right].
  \label{eq:ou-price-ito}
\end{equation}


### Parameter Estimation

For discrete observations $\{X_t\}_{t=1}^{T}$ at uniform intervals
$\Delta t$, the O-U parameters map to the AR(1) regression:

\begin{equation}
  X_t = a + b\,X_{t-1} + \eta_t,
  \qquad \eta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_\eta^2),
  \label{eq:ou-ar1}
\end{equation}

with parameter recovery via:

\begin{equation}
  \hat{\theta} = -\frac{\ln \hat{b}}{\Delta t},
  \qquad
  \hat{\mu} = \frac{\hat{a}}{1 - \hat{b}},
  \qquad
  \hat{\sigma} = \hat{\sigma}_\eta
    \sqrt{\frac{-2 \ln \hat{b}}{\Delta t\,(1 - \hat{b}^2)}}.
  \label{eq:ou-estimation}
\end{equation}


### Connection to Evolutionary Biology

\begin{remark}[O-U in phylogenetic comparative methods]
The O-U process has a rich parallel in evolutionary biology, where it models
trait evolution under stabilising selection [@hansen1997; @butler2004].
In that context, $X_t$ represents a phenotypic trait value,
$\mu$ is the optimal trait value (selective optimum), and $\theta$ quantifies
the strength of stabilising selection pulling traits toward the optimum.
The G-matrix framework for multivariate trait evolution (cf. @blomberg2012)
extends this to $\R^n$, yielding a multivariate O-U:
\begin{equation}
  d\boldsymbol{X}_t = \boldsymbol{\Theta}(\boldsymbol{\mu} - \boldsymbol{X}_t)\,dt
  + \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{1/2}\,d\boldsymbol{W}_t,
  \label{eq:ou-multivariate}
\end{equation}
where $\boldsymbol{\Theta} \in \R^{n \times n}$ is the selection strength matrix.
In our commodity price context, $\boldsymbol{\Theta}$ captures the cross-commodity
mean reversion structure---how deviations in one commodity's price influence
reversion in another.
\end{remark}


\begin{figure}[htbp]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}
\begin{axis}[
  width=12cm, height=6cm,
  xlabel={Time $t$},
  ylabel={$X_t = \log P_t$},
  axis lines=middle,
  xmin=0, xmax=10,
  ymin=-2, ymax=4,
  ytick={0, 1.5},
  yticklabels={$0$, $\mu$},
  xtick=\empty,
  every axis x label/.style={at={(ticklabel* cs:1)}, anchor=west},
  every axis y label/.style={at={(ticklabel* cs:1)}, anchor=south},
  clip=false
]

% Equilibrium line
\addplot[dashed, color=csirosteel, thick, domain=0:10] {1.5};
\node[right, color=csirosteel, font=\small] at (axis cs:10.2, 1.5)
  {equilibrium $\mu$};

% Confidence band (stationary distribution)
\addplot[fill=csiromidday, fill opacity=0.08, draw=none, domain=0:10]
  {1.5 + 1.2} \closedcycle;
\addplot[fill=white, draw=none, domain=0:10]
  {1.5 - 1.2} \closedcycle;
\addplot[dashed, color=csiromidday!40, thin, domain=0:10] {1.5 + 1.2};
\addplot[dashed, color=csiromidday!40, thin, domain=0:10] {1.5 - 1.2};

% Sample O-U path (hand-drawn approximation)
\addplot[color=csiroorange, thick, smooth]
  coordinates {
    (0, 3.5) (0.3, 3.2) (0.6, 2.8) (1.0, 2.5)
    (1.5, 2.2) (2.0, 1.8) (2.5, 1.5) (3.0, 1.7)
    (3.5, 1.3) (4.0, 0.8) (4.5, 0.5) (5.0, 0.9)
    (5.5, 1.3) (6.0, 1.6) (6.5, 1.4) (7.0, 1.8)
    (7.5, 2.2) (8.0, 1.9) (8.5, 1.5) (9.0, 1.6)
    (9.5, 1.4) (10.0, 1.5)
  };

% Annotations
\draw[{Stealth[length=2mm]}-{Stealth[length=2mm]}, thick, csiroteal]
  (axis cs:0.5, 3.2) -- (axis cs:0.5, 1.5)
  node[midway, left, font=\small, csiroteal] {$\theta(\mu - X_t)$};

\node[right, font=\scriptsize, csiromidday] at (axis cs:10.2, 2.7)
  {$\mu + \sigma/\sqrt{2\theta}$};
\node[right, font=\scriptsize, csiromidday] at (axis cs:10.2, 0.3)
  {$\mu - \sigma/\sqrt{2\theta}$};

% Starting point
\node[above right, font=\small, csiroorange] at (axis cs:0, 3.5)
  {$X_0$ (price shock)};

\end{axis}
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{Ornstein--Uhlenbeck process: a price shock at $t=0$ is gradually
pulled back toward equilibrium $\mu$ by the restoring drift
$\theta(\mu - X_t)$. The shaded band shows the stationary distribution
$\pm\sigma/\sqrt{2\theta}$ around $\mu$.}
\label{fig:ou-process}
\end{figure}


### Comparison with GBM

For completeness, geometric Brownian motion (GBM) satisfies:

\begin{equation}
  dP_t = \alpha P_t\,dt + \sigma P_t\,dW_t,
  \label{eq:gbm}
\end{equation}

with solution $P_t = P_0 \exp\bigl((\alpha - \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma^2)t
+ \sigma W_t\bigr)$. GBM has **no mean reversion**: $\E[P_t] = P_0 e^{\alpha t}$
grows (or decays) exponentially. This is appropriate for equities (which have
no natural equilibrium price) but economically inappropriate for commodities
where supply-side responses create restoring forces [@schwartz1997;
@dixit1994].


## Wavelet Decomposition {#sec-wavelets}

### Motivation

Commodity price signals contain information at multiple time scales:
short-term noise (daily/weekly fluctuations), medium-term cycles
(seasonal patterns, inventory effects), and long-term trends (structural
shifts in demand, technology, and policy). Separating these components
allows growers to answer the critical question: *"Is this price movement
real, or just noise?"*

Fourier analysis decomposes signals by frequency but sacrifices temporal
localisation. Wavelet analysis [@daubechies1992; @mallat1989] provides
**simultaneous time--frequency resolution**, making it ideal for
non-stationary economic time series.

### Continuous Wavelet Transform

\begin{definition}[Continuous Wavelet Transform]
The CWT of a signal $f \in L^2(\R)$ with respect to a mother wavelet
$\psi$ is
\begin{equation}
  \mathcal{W}_f(a, b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{|a|}}
  \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t)\,
  \psi^*\!\left(\frac{t - b}{a}\right) dt,
  \label{eq:cwt}
\end{equation}
where $a \in \R \setminus \{0\}$ is the scale (inversely related to
frequency), $b \in \R$ is the translation (time localisation), and
$\psi^*$ is the complex conjugate of $\psi$.
\end{definition}

The wavelet $\psi$ must satisfy the \emph{admissibility condition}:

\begin{equation}
  C_\psi = \int_0^{\infty}
  \frac{|\hat{\psi}(\omega)|^2}{\omega}\,d\omega < \infty,
  \label{eq:admissibility}
\end{equation}

where $\hat{\psi}$ is the Fourier transform of $\psi$. This ensures
$\psi$ has zero mean ($\int \psi(t)\,dt = 0$) and allows perfect
reconstruction.


### Discrete Wavelet Transform and Multiresolution Analysis

For computational implementation, we use the discrete wavelet transform
(DWT) based on Mallat's multiresolution analysis (MRA) framework
[@mallat1989]:

\begin{definition}[Multiresolution Decomposition]
A signal $f(t)$ is decomposed into a smooth approximation and successive
layers of detail:
\begin{equation}
  f(t) = \underbrace{\sum_{k} c_{J,k}\,\phi_{J,k}(t)}_{\text{approximation (trend)}}
  + \underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{J}\sum_{k} d_{j,k}\,\psi_{j,k}(t)}_{\text{details (fluctuations)}},
  \label{eq:mra}
\end{equation}
where:
\begin{itemize}
  \item $\phi_{J,k}(t) = 2^{-J/2}\phi(2^{-J}t - k)$ are scaled/translated
        \emph{scaling functions} (father wavelet),
  \item $\psi_{j,k}(t) = 2^{-j/2}\psi(2^{-j}t - k)$ are the
        \emph{wavelet functions} (mother wavelet) at scale $j$,
  \item $c_{J,k}$ are \emph{approximation coefficients}
        (capturing the long-term trend),
  \item $d_{j,k}$ are \emph{detail coefficients} at scale $j$
        (capturing fluctuations at time scale $\sim 2^j \Delta t$).
\end{itemize}
\end{definition}

\begin{figure}[htbp]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  node distance=0.8cm,
  box/.style={rectangle, draw=#1, fill=#1!8, rounded corners=3pt,
    minimum width=2.8cm, minimum height=0.7cm,
    font=\small, text=csiromidnight},
  arr/.style={-{Stealth[length=2.5mm]}, thick, #1}
]
% Signal
\node[box=csiromidnight] (signal) {Price signal $P_t$};

% Level 1
\node[box=csiroorange, below left=1cm and 0.5cm of signal] (a1) {$A_1$: Approx.};
\node[box=csiroteal, below right=1cm and 0.5cm of signal] (d1) {$D_1$: Detail};
\node[right=0.3cm of d1, font=\scriptsize, csirosteel] {$\sim$1--2 months};

% Level 2
\node[box=csiroorange, below left=1cm and 0.2cm of a1] (a2) {$A_2$: Approx.};
\node[box=csiroteal, below right=1cm and 0.2cm of a1] (d2) {$D_2$: Detail};
\node[right=0.3cm of d2, font=\scriptsize, csirosteel] {$\sim$3--6 months};

% Level 3
\node[box=csiroorange, below left=1cm and 0.2cm of a2] (a3) {$A_3$: Trend};
\node[box=csiroteal, below right=1cm and 0.2cm of a2] (d3) {$D_3$: Detail};
\node[right=0.3cm of d3, font=\scriptsize, csirosteel] {$\sim$6--12 months};

% Arrows
\draw[arr=csiroorange] (signal) -- (a1) node[midway, left, font=\tiny] {LP};
\draw[arr=csiroteal] (signal) -- (d1) node[midway, right, font=\tiny] {HP};
\draw[arr=csiroorange] (a1) -- (a2) node[midway, left, font=\tiny] {LP};
\draw[arr=csiroteal] (a1) -- (d2) node[midway, right, font=\tiny] {HP};
\draw[arr=csiroorange] (a2) -- (a3) node[midway, left, font=\tiny] {LP};
\draw[arr=csiroteal] (a2) -- (d3) node[midway, right, font=\tiny] {HP};

\node[below=0.3cm of a3, font=\scriptsize, csirosteel] {$>$12 months};
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{Wavelet filter bank for commodity price decomposition. At each
level, the signal is split by low-pass (LP) and high-pass (HP) filters
into approximation and detail coefficients. Detail coefficients at
different scales capture fluctuations at different time horizons, while
the final approximation captures the long-term trend.}
\label{fig:wavelet-filterbank}
\end{figure}


### Application to Commodity Prices

For monthly commodity prices, a 3-level DWT with Daubechies-4 wavelets
yields:

\begin{equation}
  P_t = \underbrace{A_3(t)}_{\substack{\text{long-term trend}\\
    \text{(structural shifts)}}}
  + \underbrace{D_3(t)}_{\substack{\text{6--12 month}\\
    \text{cycles}}}
  + \underbrace{D_2(t)}_{\substack{\text{3--6 month}\\
    \text{seasonal}}}
  + \underbrace{D_1(t)}_{\substack{\text{1--2 month}\\
    \text{noise}}}.
  \label{eq:wavelet-decomp}
\end{equation}

\begin{remark}[Grower interpretation]
A price spike in $P_t$ that appears primarily in $D_1$ is likely
short-term noise---no action required. If it appears in $D_2$ or $D_3$,
it represents a medium-term cycle that may persist for several months.
If it appears in $A_3$, it signals a fundamental structural shift.
This decomposition directly informs purchasing timing:
\textbf{buy through noise, act on trend}.
\end{remark}

### Wavelet Coherence

To examine time-varying co-movement between two commodity prices at
different scales, wavelet coherence extends the concept of correlation to
the time--frequency plane [@torrence1998]:

\begin{equation}
  R_{XY}^2(a, b) =
  \frac{\bigl|S\bigl(a^{-1}\mathcal{W}_{XY}(a,b)\bigr)\bigr|^2}
  {S\bigl(a^{-1}|\mathcal{W}_X(a,b)|^2\bigr) \cdot
   S\bigl(a^{-1}|\mathcal{W}_Y(a,b)|^2\bigr)},
  \label{eq:wavelet-coherence}
\end{equation}

where $\mathcal{W}_{XY}(a,b) = \mathcal{W}_X(a,b)\,\mathcal{W}_Y^*(a,b)$
is the cross-wavelet spectrum and $S(\cdot)$ is a smoothing operator in
both time and scale.


## Granger Causality {#sec-granger}

### Background

While cross-correlation (\S\ref{sec-ccf}) reveals lead-lag associations,
it does not test for *predictive causality*. Granger causality
[@granger1969] formalises the question: does knowledge of past values of
$X$ improve our ability to predict $Y$, beyond what $Y$'s own past provides?

\begin{definition}[Granger Causality]
$X$ is said to \emph{Granger-cause} $Y$ if
\begin{equation}
  \Var\bigl(Y_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}^{(X,Y)}\bigr) <
  \Var\bigl(Y_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}^{(Y)}\bigr),
  \label{eq:granger-def}
\end{equation}
where $\mathcal{F}_{t-1}^{(X,Y)}$ is the $\sigma$-algebra generated by
past values of both $X$ and $Y$, and $\mathcal{F}_{t-1}^{(Y)}$ by $Y$
alone.
\end{definition}

### Testing Procedure

In practice, we compare two nested regression models:

**Restricted** (only own lags):
\begin{equation}
  Y_t = \alpha_0 + \sum_{\ell=1}^{p} \alpha_\ell\, Y_{t-\ell}
  + \veps_t^{(R)}.
  \label{eq:granger-restricted}
\end{equation}

**Unrestricted** (own lags $+$ lags of $X$):
\begin{equation}
  Y_t = \alpha_0 + \sum_{\ell=1}^{p} \alpha_\ell\, Y_{t-\ell}
  + \sum_{\ell=1}^{p} \beta_\ell\, X_{t-\ell}
  + \veps_t^{(U)}.
  \label{eq:granger-unrestricted}
\end{equation}

The null hypothesis $H_0: \beta_1 = \beta_2 = \cdots = \beta_p = 0$
(no Granger causality) is tested via the F-statistic:

\begin{equation}
  F = \frac{(\text{RSS}_R - \text{RSS}_U) / p}
           {\text{RSS}_U / (T - 2p - 1)}
  \;\sim\; F(p, T - 2p - 1)
  \quad \text{under } H_0,
  \label{eq:granger-ftest}
\end{equation}

where $\text{RSS}_R$ and $\text{RSS}_U$ are the residual sums of squares
of the restricted and unrestricted models, respectively.

\begin{remark}[Interpretation for Ntracker]
Testing whether Brent crude Granger-causes urea formalises the economic
hypothesis that oil price changes propagate through the cost structure
of urea manufacturing (natural gas feedstock, transport costs). A
significant result at, say, $p = 3$ lags would statistically confirm
the 3-month early warning window identified by cross-correlation, and
provide a formal $p$-value for confidence.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Multivariate extension]
Within the VAR framework (\S\ref{sec-var}), Granger causality is tested
by imposing zero restrictions on the appropriate elements of the
coefficient matrices $\boldsymbol{A}_\ell$. Specifically, $X^{(i)}$ does not
Granger-cause $X^{(j)}$ if and only if
$[\boldsymbol{A}_\ell]_{j,i} = 0$ for all $\ell = 1, \ldots, p$
[@lutkepohl2005, Chapter 2].
\end{remark}


## Seasonal-Trend Decomposition (STL) {#sec-stl}

### Motivation

Agricultural commodity prices exhibit strong seasonal patterns driven by
planting and harvest cycles, weather, and demand fluctuations. STL
decomposition [@cleveland1990] separates these components, enabling
growers to identify **optimal purchasing windows** within the annual cycle.

\begin{definition}[Additive Decomposition]
\begin{equation}
  P_t = T_t + S_t + R_t,
  \label{eq:stl}
\end{equation}
where $T_t$ is the trend component, $S_t$ is the seasonal component
(with period $m$, typically $m = 12$ for monthly data), and $R_t$ is the
remainder (irregular component).
\end{definition}

For multiplicative relationships (common in price data where variance
scales with level), we apply the decomposition to log-prices:

\begin{equation}
  \log P_t = T_t^{(\log)} + S_t^{(\log)} + R_t^{(\log)},
  \label{eq:stl-log}
\end{equation}

which implies $P_t = e^{T_t^{(\log)}} \cdot e^{S_t^{(\log)}} \cdot
e^{R_t^{(\log)}}$---a multiplicative decomposition.

### STL Algorithm

STL uses iterated applications of locally weighted regression (loess)
to extract the components:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Inner loop} (seasonal and trend extraction):
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item Detrend: $P_t - T_t^{(k)}$
    \item Compute seasonal sub-series (group by month) and smooth with loess
    \item Low-pass filter the seasonal series to remove residual trend
    \item Subtract seasonal to get deseasonalised data
    \item Smooth with loess to obtain updated trend $T_t^{(k+1)}$
  \end{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Outer loop} (robustness weights): downweight
        outliers based on residuals $R_t = P_t - T_t - S_t$
\end{enumerate}

Key parameters:
\begin{itemize}
  \item $n_s$: seasonal smoothing window (must be odd, $\geq 7$ for
        monthly data)
  \item $n_t$: trend smoothing window (controls trend flexibility)
  \item $n_l$: low-pass filter window
\end{itemize}


### Seasonal Adjustment for Grower Purchasing

\begin{remark}[Optimal buying window]
The seasonal component $S_t$ directly reveals the typical annual price
cycle. If urea's seasonal component shows a trough in, say, June--August,
this identifies a statistically persistent purchasing window across years.
Growers can plan fertiliser procurement around these seasonal lows,
potentially saving significantly on input costs.

The remainder $R_t$ captures the unpredictable component. A large positive
$R_t$ superimposed on a seasonal low suggests an anomalous event---warranting
careful analysis before committing to large purchases.
\end{remark}


\newpage

# Synthesis and Future Directions {#sec-future}

Table~\ref{tab:methods} summarises all methods and their grower-facing
utility.

\begin{table}[htbp]
\centering
\small
\begin{tabular}{@{}p{3cm}p{4.5cm}p{4.5cm}@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{Method} & \textbf{Mathematical Basis} & \textbf{Grower Decision} \\
\midrule
Z-Score (\S\ref{sec-zscore}) &
  Standardisation of heterogeneous series &
  Compare movements across commodities \\[4pt]
Cross-Correlation (\S\ref{sec-ccf}) &
  Lagged covariance analysis &
  Early warning: which prices move first \\[4pt]
Rolling Correlation (\S\ref{sec-rolling}) &
  Windowed Pearson $\rho$ &
  Detect regime changes \\[4pt]
Percentile Alerts (\S\ref{sec-alerts}) &
  Empirical CDF &
  Traffic light: buy/sell timing \\[4pt]
VAR (\S\ref{sec-var}) &
  Multivariate autoregression &
  6-month directional forecast \\[4pt]
\midrule
O-U (\S\ref{sec-ou}) &
  Mean-reverting SDE &
  Half-life of price shocks \\[4pt]
Wavelets (\S\ref{sec-wavelets}) &
  Multiresolution analysis &
  Noise vs trend separation \\[4pt]
Granger Causality (\S\ref{sec-granger}) &
  Predictive F-testing &
  Formal causality $p$-values \\[4pt]
STL (\S\ref{sec-stl}) &
  Loess-based decomposition &
  Optimal seasonal buying windows \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Summary of analytical methods in Ntracker, with mathematical
foundations and practical grower applications. Upper block: currently
implemented. Lower block: proposed extensions.}
\label{tab:methods}
\end{table}


### Proposed Implementation Roadmap

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{STL seasonal windows}: identify optimal months for
        fertiliser purchasing via seasonal decomposition of urea prices.
  \item \textbf{Wavelet denoising}: apply DWT to separate short-term
        noise from actionable price trends in the dashboard.
  \item \textbf{O-U half-life estimation}: compute mean-reversion
        speed for each commodity, displayed as ``expected recovery time''
        after a price shock.
  \item \textbf{Granger causality testing}: add formal $p$-values to
        the lead-lag panel, strengthening the statistical evidence for
        cross-commodity predictability.
  \item \textbf{Wavelet coherence maps}: visualise time-varying
        co-movement in the time--frequency plane, revealing when and at
        what time scales commodity prices are most strongly coupled.
\end{enumerate}


# References {.unnumbered}
